Connect NEB & Co-create NEB Open Call 2027
item.description:
The EIT Community New European Bauhaus, led by EIT Urban Mobility in collaboration with EIT Food and Climate KIC, supports local innovation, citizen engagement, entrepreneurship and place-based transformation across Europe.  Through this open call, the EIT Community NEB aims to support two complementary types of local impact: 

Connect NEB is a mono-beneficiary programme that supports community-led initiatives to test new ideas, activate local communities, and experiment with participatory approaches, living labs and new forms of governance inspired by the New European Bauhaus. 

Co-create NEB is a multi-beneficiary programme that supports partnerships between communities, municipalities and other local actors to deliver visible, place-based transformation in public spaces. Proposals must be submitted by a consortium with 2 to 4 members, and at least one must be a city, a region, or an entity affiliated with a city or region.

Both programmes, each with a maximum duration of 10 months, contribute to improving the quality of life of local communities and the environments of the neighbourhoods where they are implemented, taking into account the relevance of each specific local context and valuing the innovation capacity and creativity of each community.

Despite these differences, proposals for both programmes must serve the purpose of achieving the three inseparable core values of the New European Bauhaus: Sustainable (from climate goals, to circularity, zero pollution, and biodiversity), Beautiful (quality of experience and style, beyond functionality) and Together ( from valuing diversity to equality for all, accessibility and affordability).

This Call for Proposals is open to all legal entities established in Member States of the European Union, and/or in Third countries associated to Horizon Europe. Applications from the Regional Innovation Scheme (RIS) countries are encouraged.

Applications must be submitted separately for each programme, as they are subject to different eligibility criteria, target different audiences. Applicants may apply to one or both programmes but can be eligible as the Lead Partner for only one programme.

Open Call 2: Freshwater Ecosystems
item.description:
The objective of each third-party replication project is to deploy, test, and engage developed sustainable sediment solutions within a new regional freshwater ecosystem type (lotic, lentic, transitional/coastal, or anthropogenic). Every selected project must execute a mandatory work plan divided into specific tasks over three programmatic phases: Project Definition (Months 1–2), Project Implementation (Months 3–6), and Project Evaluation (Months 7–9).

Core tasks across these phases include:

  1. Developing a localised baseline assessment, regional water basin characterisation, and local stakeholder mapping database.
  2. Executing targeted regional monitoring activities from the designated catalogue, focusing on flow monitoring, sediment quantity monitoring, and/or rapid ecotoxicological sediment quality assessments.
  3. Conducting active community stakeholder engagement, multi-actor feedback sessions, and environmental awareness campaigns to co-design future measure protocols.
  4. Formulating a comprehensive Regional Strategic Action Plan and Implementation Roadmap with indicative budgets and local timelines to upscale and replicate sustainable practices across other EU basins.

Technical Mentoring Support

Throughout the entire nine-month duration, selected associated regions will receive dedicated, direct technical support from assigned technical partners and project mentors. This provision includes up to eight (8) hours of individual technical mentoring per month per funded project. Mentors will work closely with your local team from the onboarding phase to co-define your project specifications, establish your individual budget and indicators, monitor your practical pilot implementation progress, and facilitate cross-border data harmonisation with the wider consortium network.

EXTENDED: Financial Support to Third Parties: SWIM Open Call for Associated Regions
item.description:
The selected beneficiaries will develop tailored replication roadmaps for the restoration, protection, and enhancement of freshwater and transitional ecosystems, with a particular focus on habitats of migratory fish species, ensuring the future replication of SWIM solutions. They will join the SWIM Community of Practice (CoP) and the SWIM Alliance, collaborating with other regions, partners, and stakeholders.

Activities to be carried out may include:

  1. Improvement of habitat connectivity and quality for migratory fish species.
  2. Monitoring and rehabilitation of freshwater ecosystems and migratory fish habitats.
  3. Data collection (historical data sets, water quality assessments, species surveys).
  4. Actions to raise awareness about fish migration barriers and ecosystem degradation.
  5. Engagement of local communities, fisheries communities, and relevant stakeholders.
  6. Planning of future actions for habitat restoration and sediment management.
  7. Development of implementation plans on a larger scale, aligned with SWIM pilot activities.
  8. Identification of funding sources for future implementation actions.
  9. Contribution to SWIM Alliance activities and knowledge exchange.

item.title: AI Challenge Competition
item.description:
> Challenge 3:  Generative AI for Enhancement of Clinical Datasets

Led by EUropean Federation for CAncer IMages (EUCAIM), this challenge focuses on using Generative AI to enhance the quality and representativeness of clinical imaging datasets, which are often incomplete or imbalanced. By creating realistic synthetic patient cohorts, the solution aims to fill critical data gaps and harmonise information across various imaging conditions. The system will identify key demographic and clinical characteristics to ensure synthetic data remains realistic and consistent with real-world statistics. Ultimately, the project seeks to improve fairness and reduce bias, enabling the development of trustworthy models that support accurate medical research and clinical decision-making.


> Challenge 4: Generative AI for Automatic Test Case Generation from Crash Databases & Standards

Led by Siemens Industry Software NV with the collaboration of EU RobustifAI, this challenge aims to enhance the safety and validation of autonomous driving systems by using Generative AI to automate the creation of simulation scenarios. By transforming accident reports, visual data, and international safety standards into structured, simulation-ready formats, the solution replaces slow, manual processes with a more scalable and consistent workflow. This approach strengthens the link between real-world data and regulatory frameworks, helping teams identify critical safety gaps and complex edge cases. Ultimately, the project will provide technical and regulatory experts with comprehensive scenario sets, significantly improving the efficiency and accessibility of safety assessments for autonomous vehicles.

item.title: Community Grant – Citizen Solutions to Sustainability Challenges
item.description:
Community grants can be awarded for activities that support the objectives of the Urban Prosumers project and produce one or more of the following results:

  1. Practices, services or models related to sustainable mobility, energy and/or food that benefit people or communities in Tampere
  2. Technological innovations that enable people to both produce and use sustainable solutions
  3. New funding models for communities’ climate actions
  4. New operating models for residents’ participation in the production and use of sustainable solutions
  5. New information on what prevents or enables people’s participation in the production and use of sustainable solutions
  6. New forms of cooperation for climate actions between residents and local actors
  7. Business opportunities and ideas for the Tampere region related to sustainable mobility, energy or food


The sustainability solutions to be funded can be implemented in different forms, such as new services that support sustainable mobility, energy and/or the food economy, digital applications, civic education models and training materials, and the development of forms of cooperation between residents and the City of Tampere.


Please note that the solutions and experiments implemented during the funded community projects must take place in Tampere or focus on Tampere. The aim is that community projects benefit a wider group of people than the applicant’s own community.

Open Call for the Selection of Providers of Innovative MRV technologies
item.description:
The activities that qualify for financial support include:

• The development and implementation of technologies for measuring soil health indicators such as water retention, organic matter, microbial biodiversity, among others.

• The installation and maintenance of innovative equipment and technologies at the experimentation sites.

• Training farmers and Living Lab teams on the use of these technologies to maximise their impact on soil monitoring.

• Reporting results and analysing the data collected during two agricultural seasons.

• Collaboration with Farmers and Engagement of Actors in the Living Labs: Technology providers must actively engage with farmers and LL stakeholders to test and refine their MRV solutions. This includes on farm testing, training sessions, and feedback loops to ensure usability and effectiveness in real-world Conditions.

MRV Harmonization Across Living Labs

Given that different Living Labs may employ distinct MRV technologies, ensuring data comparability and harmonization is a key priority. The following strategies will be implemented by the consortium members:

• Standardized Data Collection Protocols: Regardless of the MRV technology used, all LLs will adhere to a common framework for data collection, ensuring that core soil health indicators (e.g., soil organic carbon, microbial activity, water retention) are measured in a standardized manner. 

• Interoperability Requirements for MRV Technologies: The technologies developed across different LLs should provide interoperability and data-sharing capabilities, allowing seamless integration of different technologies into a unified data analysis framework. 

• Cross-LL Calibration and Validation: Periodic inter-Living Lab validation exercises will be conducted to benchmark and harmonize data from different MRV tools, ensuring consistency across regions and experimental conditions.

CR.EU.IN.HERITAGE: Cultural Heritage Innovation Grants – STEP 2
item.description:
Applicant projects, that passed Step 1 evaluation (Results of Step 1 are available at the following link: Step 1 Results), are invited to submit proposals for collaborative innovation projects specifically focused on the Cultural Heritage Value Chain (CHVC). Projects must contribute to European strategic autonomy and the twin transition (green and digital).

The Call provides two distinct funding strands, both directly applied to the CHVC:

  1. Strand 1. Product Innovation
  2. Strand 2. Process Innovation

Proposals must build on a Step 1-selected idea, maintaining the previously selected strand.

For further information, please check the full Call Package.


Key Requirements:

  1. Consortium: The Step 2 proposal must be submitted by a consortium of at least two SMEs from eligible SMP countries. While Lead Partner must be a Step 1-selected SME, Project Partners may include SMEs not selected in Step 1, if they meet general eligibility. Associated Partners may be added without EU funding (their involvement does not count towards the minimum eligibility requirements of this Call).
  2. Technology: Projects must demonstrate the application of EU-based digital solutions/technologies (Option A) and/or the engagement of Innovation Leaders (Option B). Access the Innovation Leaders catalogue to browse the listed stakeholders. Innovation Leaders may be engaged as:
  3. an applicant SME within the consortium (if the Innovation Leader qualifies as an SME and meets all eligibility requirements of this Call).
  4. an external entity (either as an Associated Partner, or as a Service Provider)
  5. Duration: The project implementation period is fixed at 12 months.
  6. Co-financing requirement: the EU Grant covers up to 85% of the total project budget; therefore consortia must provide at least 15% co-financing. This contribution is mandatory for all participating SMEs within a project. The co-financing may be covered directly by SMEs' own resources or by external private funding. While 15% is the minimum requirement, providing a higher percentage of co-financing will result in additional points during the evaluation process.
  7. Other financial aspects: the financial support provided under this Call is a Grant structured as a lump sum, meaning that payments are linked to the achievement of project milestones and the approval of technical reports. While the grant will be managed according to a lump sum approach, in Step 2 the budget must be submitted based on real costs using the mandatory templates in Annex 5 or Annex 6, so that evaluators can assess how the project budget is structured, whether it is coherent with the proposed activities, and how the required co-financing will be covered. The costs included in the budget must be incurred in the project period, be connected with the action and necessary for its implementation, comply with applicable national laws, be reasonable and justified and comply with the principle of sound financial management. VAT is not eligible and therefore should not be included as an eligible cost.

Mandatory annexes to be submitted in Step 2: the following mandatory annexes must be submitted through the platform, in line with the application procedure described in the Call document. Call annexes are available at this link.

  1. Annex 4 - Step 2 Application Form: duly completed and signed by all applicants (the form includes a Declaration of Honour (DoH) that must be signed by the legal representative of each SME requesting funding).
  2. Annex 5 or Annex 6 - Step 2 Budget Template: Applicants should fill Annex 5 or 6 depending on the selected strand (Strand 1 or Strand 2).
  3. Financial viability self-assessment: completed using the self-assessment tool, printed and signed for each SME requesting funding. Follow the instructions provided in Annex 2.
  4. Proof of legal registration: for each SME requesting funding. E.g., company register extract, certificate of incorporation, or equivalent official registration document. Provide English translation where applicable (see the Call document).
  5. Balance sheet, and Profit and loss account for the last two closed financial years: for each SME requesting funding. Provide English translation where applicable (see the Call document).
  6. Mandatory questionnaire: mandatory questionnaire available at this link. Filled in by the Lead Partner and saved as a pdf document.

item.title: DIGITWIN4CIUE European Excellence Scholarship 2026-2027-3000€
item.description:
Scholarship Compatibility Policy

The DIGITWIN4CIUE project-Scholarships for the Executive Master in Digital Twins on Infrastructures and Cities are compatible with any other DIGITWIN4CIUE project scholarships. These scholarships are co-[inanced (50%) by the European Union.

However, other scholarships are not compatible if their combinedtotal exceeds the full cost ofthe master’s program,nor may they be used for any purpose other than the one originally intended.

Additionally, the scholarships offered in this call are not compatible with other tuition fee scholarships, unless explicitly stated otherwise in the call for the other scholarship.

Further information Legal disclaimer:

This call (as well as the financial support that will be providedthrough it) is NOT an EU action. This call is launched solely by Fundación Agustín de Betancourt (FUNAB). The financial support to the selected successful applicants will be provided directlyby FUNAB in its own name and under its own responsibility. FUNAB's own responsibility includes the preparation and drafting of this call as well as the selection of successful applicants that will receive the said financial support. The publication of this call in the EU Funding & Tenders Portal is made solelyfor information purposeswith the aim to increasethe visibility of this call to the general public, including to potential applicants (i.e. students). The said publication shall not be construedas direct or indirect approvalof the content and conditions of this call by the relevant EU authorities or as any form of waiver from the above-described responsibility of FUNAB.

Open Call of Perform Europe 2026 – 2028
1st Open Call round – Supporting SMEs in the Textile Sector towards Digital and Green Transition
item.description:
Link to the online Application Portal : https://ec.europa.eu/eusurvey/runner/FutureProofTextiles-1st-OpenCall

Open Call objective: Empowering 20 SMEs in the textile sector to successfully navigate the "twin transitions" of digital transformation and green sustainability.

Support: Beyond the €60,000 co-financing grant (50 000 EUR – fixed lump Sum Grant, 10 000 EUR – required self-funding by SME), FutureProofTextiles offers extensive capacity-building and networking resources to help build a connected SME ecosystem:

  1. Tailored Training: An average of 40 hours per SME focusing on digital transformation, circular economy principles, and sustainable production.
  2. Dedicated Mentoring: Custom technical consulting and mentoring sessions for up to 6 selected SMEs based on specific needs.
  3. Open-Access Materials: Production of 100 hours of scalable training curricula and best-practice guides for the broader textile sector.
  4. Cross-Border Networking: Facilitation of international collaboration to foster an innovative textile ecosystem.

(Further details regarding these services will be shared with the selected SMEs.)

Who can apply:

Company size

  1. Applicants must fall under the category of SME, according toEuropean Commission Recommendation 2003/361/EC*
  2. Partnerships Clusters and intermediary organisations are not eligible even if they are SMEs.
  3. Each applicant should represent a single SME, not a consortia of multiple SMEs.

Industry

  1. Applicants must be SMEs operating in the textile value chain. SMEs from complementary sectors (e.g., tech, machinery) are also eligible, provided their proposed innovation directly contributes to the textile value chain. Activities must align with a relevant NACE code (or national equivalent), such as:
  2. Textile manufacturing: C13.1, C13.2, C13.3, C13.9
  3. Apparel manufacturing: C14.1, C14.3
  4. Textile-based products and technical textiles: C15.12, C22.19
  5. Textile recycling and circularity: C38
  6. Digital and innovative solutions for the textile sector: J62, J63
  7. Manufacture of machinery for textile, apparel and leather production:C 28.94

Location

  1. Only SMEs legally established in countries participating in the Single Market Programme (SMP) are eligible to apply. This includes EU Member States and any other country formally associated with the SMP. A full list of participating countries is published here: List of participating countries in the Single Market Programme (SMP)

Language

  1. The applicant SME must have at least one staff member who speaks English and will be actively involved in the implementation process, handling all communication needs with the consortium.

Submission Rules:

Platform

  1. Proposal submitted online on the EU Survey Platform. Any other means will not be accepted. Application Form

Language

  1. Proposals must be written in English. Submissions done in any other language will not be evaluated.

Submission Limit

  1. Applicant SMEs may submit up to two proposals per Open Call round (one under Green Transition and one under Digital Innovations). Despite this, each SME is eligible to receive a maximum of one grant award across the entire program.

Proposal Requirements

  1. The proposal must demonstrate a Technology Readiness Level (TRL) of 5 or higher.

Compliance & Ethics

Applicants must confirm their full compliance with all rules, including absence of double funding, and conflict of interest, by reviewing and signing the mandatory forms included in the application:

  1. Funding eligibility and absence of conflict of interest declaration form
  2. SME and Eligibility declaration form

Step by Step Guide

Applicants may submit one proposal for a Green Transition project and one for a Digital Innovation project. Please note that a separate application must be submitted for each.

Step 1: Verify Eligibility

Review the eligibility requirements thoroughly to ensure your organization and project qualify for funding.

Step 2: Concept & Budgeting

  1. Check out “Exhaustive List of Eligible Activities and Eligible Costs” document.
  2. Check out “Needs of the SMEs in the Textile Sector” document, which helps the brainstorming process.
  3. Brainstorm your proposal. When brainstorming, keep in mind that the project implementation period is 9months.
  4. Compose an initial draft, and develop a comprehensive budget based on eligible costs. Clearly define your objectives by establishing specific milestones.

Step 3: Draft Responses

  1. Prepare your answers for each section of the “Standardized Application Form Template” document, before starting the online submission.

Step 4: Prepare Mandatory Documents

Complete and sign the following required forms:

  1. Complete the “Tables with Activities, Budget, Milestones, and Risks” document. Please note that milestones may be subject to negotiation if the proposal is selected, and applicants will not be evaluated based on the proposed milestones
  2. Fill in the “SME and Eligibility Declaration form”.
  3. Fill in the “Funding eligibility and absence of conflict of interest declaration form”.

Step 5: Complete the Online Form

Access the online portal – EU Survey to input your responses. We recommend using a stable internet connection to prevent data loss.

Step 6: Upload Attachments

Attach all mandatory documents from Step 4 into their respective upload fields within the form.

All necessary documents are available for downloading through the Application portal and through the relevant posts in projects LindeIn and ECCP page.

item.title: AI Challenge Competition
item.description:
> Challenge 1: GenAI-Based Natural Language Mission Generator for Autonomous Robots in Agriculture _ Intellimech

Led by Consorzio Intellimech and JOiINT LAB, this challenge aims to democratise robotic programming by using Generative AI to translate natural language into executable commands.

By leveraging advanced Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, participants will develop a modular Proof of Concept (TRL 5) that bridges the gap between human intent and robotic execution. While the initial focus is on enhancing vineyard productivity, the solution is designed for broad application across industrial maintenance and field operations. This challenge seeks to accelerate intelligent automation throughout Europe by making sophisticated robotics accessible to non-technical operators.

> Challenge 2: Agentic AI for Automated CAD Generation and Autonomous Simulation _ SIAD Group

Led by SIAD Group, this challenge focuses on developing an agentic AI system to streamline the design and simulation of industrial piping, specifically for routing compressor tubes within skids. By translating natural language requirements and existing CAD data into editable parametric models, the solution aims to automate complex tasks such as extracting geometric constraints, mesh generation, and convergence analysis. Ultimately, the project seeks to significantly reduce manual engineering effort, optimise layouts for compactness, and lower costs, ensuring the final output is validated against stringent industrial standards in real-world environments.

UPSTREAM Open Call 2: Prevention & Zero‑Waste
item.description:
Eligible activities (exhaustive list for the calls)

Activity focus: Prevention and Zero-Waste on rivers

Projects must demonstrably support UPSTREAM objectives through: 1) Monitoring & digitalisation of plastics/MP in rivers & tributaries; 2) Prevention & zero‑waste measures (e.g., substitution with bio‑based/biodegradable alternatives, upstream interventions); 3) Removal/collection & recovery of litter, plastics and microplastics in freshwater systems; 4) Recycling/valorisation pilots for captured plastics (incl. pre‑treatments that enable recycling); 5) Nature‑based solutions & restoration in riparian and urban water systems; 6) Citizen science & co‑creation (living labs, behaviour‑change campaigns) that directly support the above; 7) Open data & knowledge sharing with UPSTREAM’s platforms.

Activities sought. Substitution & procurement pilots; zero‑waste supply chain measures; behaviour‑change and producer responsibility pilots; enabling policies; monitoring impacts

Not eligible: Basic research; routine municipal operations; activities completed before grant start; double‑funded activities; purchases unrelated to project tasks; measures with significant negative environmental impacts.

Deliverables, KPIs & reporting (for grantees)

Mandatory deliverables: - D1: Kick‑off package (work plan, risk & ethics checklist, data plan, comms plan) – M1; - D2: Mid‑term update (evidence of activities, preliminary results) – mid‑point; - D3: Final pack (technical report, datasets, photos/videos, replication & exploitation plan, communication assets) – final month.

Illustrative KPIs (select per project): capture efficiency; tonnes/items collected; MP concentration trends; citizens engaged; policy changes enacted; % bio‑based substitutions; valorisation outputs; open datasets published.

Visibility. Use MISSION OCEAN/EU/UPSTREAM logos and funding statement on all outputs; provide consented media assets; follow project visual ID.

Open data. Publish non‑sensitive results to UPSTREAM platforms/Portal as applicable.

item.title: Support for the Integration of Post-Quantum Cryptography into Application Platforms
item.description:
Project scope

The objective of the call is to support all categories of companies that integrate post-quantum cryptography into application platforms. The supported projects should help organisations prepare for future quantum-related cybersecurity risks by analysing existing cryptographic mechanisms, identifying suitable applications for migration, documenting application architecture and data flows, designing the replacement of cryptographic algorithms, and preparing a migration plan.

Mandatory activities include risk analysis based on the assessment of cryptographic mechanisms, identification and selection of an application suitable for migration to post-quantum cryptographic mechanisms, description of the application architecture, documentation of implemented cryptographic mechanisms, proposal for replacing cryptographic algorithms, and preparation of a project plan.

Optional activities may include implementation of post-quantum cryptography, testing and updating the application architecture. If optional activities are selected, all optional activities must be implemented, and the applicant must demonstrate sufficient internal or external capacity to carry out the implementation.

item.title: ZMINA:Resilience. Rolling call for International Showcasing of co-creation projects
MANTRA Second Open Call for Manufacturing SMEs
item.description:
The objective of this call is to select up to 25 manufacturing SMEs that will receive support to define and implement their MANTRA Journey plan, with the aim of becoming more sustainable and innovative.

Through this initiative, selected SMEs will enhance their capacity to adopt advanced technologies and social innovation for sustainability through collaboration with technology providers and by accessing services provided by EU specialised business support networks and organisations. Financial support of up to EUR 50,000 per SME will be available to implement the recommended transformation roadmap following the advisory phase.

Proposals should focus on a transformation project that is realistic, impactful and embedded in the SME’s strategy for long-term sustainability and growth through the uptake of advanced technologies. The proposed concept must contribute to the green and digital transformation of the SME by reducing environmental impacts while enhancing the competitiveness and resilience of the business. Projects should align with circular economy principles and consider inclusive and socially responsible practices.

Selected SMEs will participate in the MANTRA Journey, consisting of a six-month advisory phase followed by a six-month implementation phase supported through MANTRA financial assistance.

Your journey begins by joining the MANTRA Community, which serves as your gateway to resources, peer learning, networking opportunities and access to the application process.

item.title: Call for Proposals for the Sustainable Management of Caribbean OCTs’ Natural Capital
SMART ERA 2nd Open Call for Followers Micro-Pilots
item.description:
SMART ERA is an EU-funded project that aims to transform rural areas by addressing their socio-economic and environmental challenges through advanced technology and innovative solutions.

SMART ERA impacts will span across technology, governance, business, society, and policymaking, fostering sustainable development and growth.

The six (6) Pilot Regions leading the way are:

  1. Val di Sole/Trentino, Italy
  2. Tramuntana/Sóller, Spain 
  3. Northern Ostrobothnia, Finland
  4. Trebinje/East Herzegovina, Bosnia and Herzegovina
  5. Šmarje-Padna, Slovenia, Italy, Croatia
  6. Devetaki Plateau, Bulgaria

They will become smart villages through the application of Smart Innovation Packages (SIPs) co-designed and validated with local communities. Joint efforts can revitalize rural communities, combat depopulation, and create resilient, vibrant societies.

The SMART ERA consortium brings together 25 organisations from 10 countries, counting with the adequate resources for achieving the project objectives. 

With this 2nd Open Call for Followers Micro-Pilots, SMART ERA aims to transfer the project's results, methodology, and validation strategies to four (4) new "Follower Regions", engaging new local communities to prove the replicability and impact potential of the SMART ERA approach.


The SMART ERA 2nd Open Call for Followers Micro-Pilots will select up to 4 Proposals to establish 4 Followers Micro-Pilots. These Micro-Pilots will replicate the SMART ERA methodology by identifying regional needs and co-designing new Smart Innovation Packages (SIP). 

The total maximum funding for one Follower Micro-Pilot depends on the application format: 

  1. If applying as a Consortium total lump sum up to €90,000, distributed as follows:
  2. Community Activator: Up to €30,000.
  3. Pilot Orchestrator (Technical Provider): Up to €60,000.
  4. If applying as an Individual (covering both roles):
  5. Total lump sum: Up to €60,000.

Each team selected under the SMART ERA 2nd Open Call will join a 12-month Support Programme with the mentoring from SMART ERA Technical Partners. 

The Support Programme includes the following stages:

  1. Stage 1: On-boarding Stage, during which the beneficiary should deliver an Individual Mentoring Plan (including planned activities, KPIs, milestones and budget). The duration of this stage is up to 2 months.
  2. Stage 2: Mobilisation Stage, during which the Report on Community Engagement should be delivered. The duration of this stage is up to 3 months.
  3. Stage 3: Deployment Stage, which should lead to a Technical Solution Completed. The duration of this stage is up to 7 months.

At the end of the Support Programme, the selected teams will have developed:

  1. A design for a Smart Innovation Package
  2. A technological solution, as the center of the SIP, developed and tested.

NEB Academy | Skills Infrastructure
item.description:
The NEB Academy | Skills Infrastructure call supports consortia that develop, adapt and deliver regional skills infrastructures and learning pathways contributing to the sustainable transformation of the lived environment.

Projects must address identified regional skills gaps, integrate New European Bauhaus values and creative practices, build on existing initiatives and legacy assets, and deliver EIT-labelled, non-labelled and other learning activities for relevant target groups. The call aims to create scalable educational frameworks that embed sustainability, inclusivity and aesthetics, while supporting long-term regional systemic transformation.

Selected projects are expected to contribute to a coherent NEB Academy skills ecosystem and generate long-term educational, societal and market impact.

item.title: NEB Academy | Fashion Shift
item.description:
The NEB Academy | Fashion Shift call supports the transformation of the European fashion industry by funding collaborative consortia that develop and scale circular, regenerative and responsible business models grounded in the New European Bauhaus values of sustainability, inclusion and beauty.

Projects should build on existing businesses, pilots, initiatives or proven concepts that are ready to be developed, deployed or scaled through meaningful collaboration. Activities may focus on different parts of the fashion value chain, including design, material sourcing, production, distribution and end-of-life management, and should generate measurable environmental, social, societal and market impact.

The call aims to bridge the gap between creative vision and market reality, supporting a more sovereign European fashion ecosystem moving beyond fast fashion through shared responsibility, innovation and cross-sector collaboration.

item.title: EUDOROS Open Call #2
item.description:
The EUDOROS Open Call #2 projects will increase the cybesecurity preparedness of organisations within the EU.

They are expected to follow a three-stage structure:

(1) Planning (2 months),

(2) Usage, Adoption & Consultancy (5 months), and

(3) Evaluation & Feedback (3 months).

Throughout these 10 months, selected entities will implement, test, and refine the EUDOROS preparedness support services, and possibly other cybersecurity solutions, within their own infrastructure, supported by consultancy, training, and technical guidance from the EUDOROS project partners.

item.title: Promoting civil society contribution to good local governance and sustainable local economies
RIZA Call for proposals
BIO-INSPIRE Teaming & Knowledge Exchange Programme Open Call
item.description:
The initiative aims to boost innovation capacity and foster knowledge transfer, particularly among bio-based actors from BIO-INSPIRE less advanced regions, by enabling exchanges and study visits with organisations from other European regions sharing common thematic and technological interests.

The call allocates a total budget of €300,000 to fund 30 projects, each receiving a €10,000 grant and access to a 6-month collaboration and knowledge exchange programme.

The Open Call will be open to any legal entity established in a Horizon Europe eligible country (See section 2 in the Guidelines for applicants), applying as a consortium of up to three partners maximum.

Each application must define a consortium of two roles:

  1. Guest entity (Coordinator): the coordinating organisation must be established in a BIO-INSPIRE region (Greece, Hungary, Slovakia, Lithuania, Romania, Bulgaria or Poland) and will carry out the main guest activities.
  2. Host entity: organisations established in different regions (advanced or less represented regions) that will host the exchange activities.

All participating entities must be established in different regions, ensuring genuine cross-regional collaboration and knowledge exchange.

All members of the Teaming & Knowledge Exchange Programme consortium applying to the BIO-INSPIRE Open Call will be referred to as ‘Third Parties (TPs)’, and their activities within BIO-INSPIRE will be referred to as ‘subprojects’.

The programme will be implemented in two stages:

  1. Stage 1 – Plan Phase (up to 2 months): Beneficiaries will develop and submit a Research & Innovation Plan, outlining key challenges, planned bio-based innovation activities and budget.
  2. Stage 2 – Exchange Phase (4 months): This phase will focus on the implementation of the planned activities, including a on-site visit at the hosting organisation. The stage will conclude with a Technical Report and a 2-year R&I Collaboration Plan, laying the foundations for long-term cooperation beyond the programme.

Digital4Business Scholarships Call – Joint European Master’s in Advanced Digital Technologies for Business (September 2026 Intake)
item.description:
Under Task 4.1 of the Grant Agreement, 100 scholarships will be offered for the September 2026 intake, targeting learners facing socioeconomic barriers, underrepresentation in STEM, or geographic disparities.

Alongside the free September 2026 intake, this scholarship scheme serves as a structured inclusion mechanism to ensure vulnerable groups are prioritised and supported.

Scholarships are awarded based on eligibility criteria and evaluation under the Equity, Diversity and Inclusion (EDI) framework, rather than academic merit, ensuring fair access for those facing barriers in traditional admissions. They also provide fully funded, industry-recognised certifications to enhance skills and employability in line with labour market needs.

Overall, the scheme supports diversity, reduces inequalities, and advances digital skills development across Europe.

NGI Fediversity open call (2026-08F)
item.description:
Note that this programme is building a hosting stack based on the Nix functional package manager. if your project is not related to Nix or to high availability hosting, we suggest you submit to another open call from NLnet such as Restack. Please also note that there is a policy on the use of AI, and that any submission involving AI involves providing a prompt log. All generated content should be marked as such, even if subsequently edited.

Our software supply chains are a silent miracle, the result of millions of hours spent every year packaging, compiling and distributing software. From this vast firehose of bits come the apps and services we depend on every day. Subsequently, billions of hours are spent by users on dealing with everything from simple updates to complex system administration. This vast effort is necessary to keep oneself abreast of the never-ending supply of potential vulnerabilities that arrives with just a slight delay. Users that fail to responsively track new releases of every deployed service and each of its dependencies, are bound to land themselves or others in trouble sooner rather than later.

Deploying internet based services is much tougher than we can afford. No matter how user friendly or well-written software is, enabling the community at large to maintain internet services by themselves at scale has proven to be consistently challenging. Achieving high availability scenario's is even more of a dark art. No wonder, people just give in and surrender their data.

Fediversity is a comprehensive effort to bring easy-to-use, hosted online services with service portability and personal freedom at their core to everyone. It wants to provide everyone with high-quality, secure IT systems for everyday use. Without tracking, without exploitation, in a way that runs everywhere and scales effortlessly. Fediversity is based on NixOS, a disruptive Linux distribution with a unique approach to package and configuration management. Built on top of the Nix package manager, NixOS is completely declarative, makes upgrading systems reliable, and has many other advantages. Because it is reproducible, it is ideally suited for complex deployment scenario's where consistent behaviour, stability and configurability matter.

One such "complex" deployment scenario is running state-of-the-art services for the Fediverse, like PeerTube, Mastodon, Owncast or Lemmy — especially if you want to for instance add services like live chat or transcoding. But even running more traditional services like modern e-mail servers with possible whistles and bells can be daunting. The same holds for deploying a VPN, private cloud storage, wiki, etc. Fediversity will enable all of these use cases, and more — finally bringing these to the market in a way that is as conveient as using a hosted service.

Fediversity will deliver an ambitious development effort, but this is a vast domain with many more challenges than what any preconceived effort could tackle by itself. This is why we invite your contribution to help us reshape the state of play, and together create an open, trustworthy and reliable internet for all.

This is your opportunity to make a real difference. You tell us how your project can help Fediversity go harder, better, faster, stronger. In order to enable you to make such contributions, we will award small to medium-size R&D grants towards solutions that bring the next generation of mobile solutions closer. We are seeking project proposals between 5.000 and 50.000 euro's — which should get you on your way. If the project works out well you can subsequently scale up in other programmes funded by NLnet such as NGI0 Commons Fund. Noteworthy fact: many projects which are to be deployed inside Fediversity were themselves bootstrapped on precisely such a grant from NGI, and now it is your turn.

item.title: Strengthening and Expanding Living Labs with Additional Experimental Sites
item.description:
The implementation of all selected projects will be structures into three phases over 36 months. Each phase will conclude with the submission of a report summarising the work completed during that phase:

Phase 1 – Early implementation and baseline phase (M1-M12): Upfront payment of 70% of the total fund (prefinancing); initial inception and engagement report (M12)

Phase 2 – Co-solution testing and assessment phase (M13-M24): Payment of 15% of the total fund; Intermediate progress report (M24)

Phase 3 – Final implementation phase (M25-M36): Payment of final 15% of the total fund; Final implementation report (M36)

Once submitted, each report will be reviewed by the Monitoring Committee. A request for revision may be issued with specific requirements for what should be modified. When the deliverable is officially accepted, payments will be released.

Selected applicants will cooperate closely with the Nemesis consortium and will be fully integrated into the co-creation and overall implementation processes. They will become active actors within their respective LLs and will benefit from the activities and knowledge generated by the project, while building capacity and co-implementing solutions at their experimental sites, contributing data and participating in the LL activities and initiatives.

The exact milestones, reporting obligations and payment conditions will be specified in the sub-grant agreement. Selected recipients will also be required to comply with obligations regarding conflict of interest, data sharing, FAIR principles, data protection, ethics, communication and dissemination, information obligations and record-keeping.

item.title: NGI TALER open call (2026-08T)
item.description:
In the digital economy, payments play a critical role. Yet online payment systems tend to allow for far less privacy than paying with a bank note or coins, especially when using proprietary solutions like Google Pay or Apple Pay. When interacting with the offline economy comes into play, the alternative of paying with all kind of volatile cryptocurrencies isn't a viable option either.


NGI TALER is a project funded by the European Commission and the Swiss State to roll out a new electronic payment system that benefits everyone: people, merchants, banks, financial authorities, auditors and anti-corruption researchers. The project doesn't have to start from scratch either, but builds on the strong foundations of GNU Taler — the privacy-preserving digital payment system developed by the GNU community and Taler Systems SA. This offers privacy for those that make payments, while enforcing transparency on those that sell. By providing micro payments at very low overhead, GNU Taler permits internet business models to shift away from advertising revenue or subscription models, especially for online publishers. No-risk transactions can lower transaction fees and open online payments for the underbanked population and citizens marginalized from digitalisation.


Part of the budget of NGI TALER (15% to be precise) is reserved for open calls to fund additional free and open source efforts that are aligned with the topics and approach of NGI TALER. We invite your contributions to help reshape the state of play of digital payment systems, and to help create an open, trustworthy and reliable internet for all. Of course you can contribute exciting new capabilities to GNU Taler itself, build auxiliary tools or work on user experience, but you could also be developing integrations into FOSS applications and open standards (enabling P2P micropayments in for instance an instant messenger, open social media platform or video conferencing tool), or work on improvements to infrastructure components like merchant backends. Together with the rest of the Next Generation Internet initiative, we move towards restoring and subsequently maintaining European sovereignty and to secure democratic ownership of our digital societies.


In order to enable you to make such contributions, we will award small to medium-size R&D grants towards technology commons that empower users, and establish a new generation of privacy-friendly digital payments systems. The programme will run between now and November 2026, but budgets are limited — so grab your opportunity.


Project results must be made available under a free and open source license, so anyone can read and validate the source code, and anyone can use the code to create technology that fits their own purposes. We are seeking project proposals between 5.000 and 50.000 euro's — if the project works out well you can subsequently scale up in other programmes funded by NLnet such as NGI0 Commons Fund. Reliable, low-cost, secure and resource efficient payments should become the 'new normal' of the internet, something ordinary users should not have to worry about — users should be in control. So let's make it happen. Note that GNU Taler itself was funded through such Next Generation Internet projects, so there is every opportunity to grow.


Through the open calls, we assist independent researchers and developers to join in and create powerful new technologies and put these in the hands of future generations as building blocks for a fair and democratic society. Help us build a sustainable and open economy that benefits all, and submit your idea today. Not sure whether your idea fits? Don't be afraid to send something completely out-of-the-box if you think you can contribute to the topic (and it fits with the eligibility criteria): it really is an open call.


Want to learn more about Taler before you submit a proposal, or have other questions? Take a look at the GNU Taler technology, the main NGI Taler site - or join the GNU Taler Integration Community Hub to have a chat about your ideas.

item.title: RIS Education Open Call 2027
item.description:
The RIS Education Open Call 2027 aims at funding projects that can support the overall RIS strategy and aims, offer high-quality education, reach a large audience and market, and gain recognition efficiently with a lasting effect. The EIT Urban Mobility RIS Education objectives are focused on capacity building in innovation and entrepreneurship and urban mobility. These objectives are reflected in the activity types proposed in this call. The activities responding to these objectives should aim to improve transferrable skills that will lead to further innovation and entrepreneurship in urban mobility in RIS countries.   

There are five general topics (RISE 1-5) that can be addressed by the proposals: 

RISE1: Capacity-Building in Innovation and Entrepreneurship 

RISE2: Education and Training Activities in Urban Mobility 

RISE3: Organisation of Winter and Summer Schools 

RISE4: Scaling up of Local, Regional and National Initiatives 

RISE5: Network building with educational players

item.title: C4B Open Call for Pilot Projects
item.description:
Selected projects will be launched in January 2027 having an implementation timeframe of up to 12 months (ending no later than December 2027).

Expected Activities

Applicants are expected to propose a Pilot Project that demonstrates in real cases the feasibility of a C4B bio-based Business Model.

Mandatory activities

  1. Tailoring a C4B business model to a specific business case (e.g. Developing a specific business case and governance structure on the basis of business models developed in the C4B project).
  2. Assessing the feasibility of the business model, including at least two of the following:
  3. Assessment of the technological feasibility.
  4. Assessment of the governance structure and fairness.
  5. Assessment of economic feasibility, market research.
  6. Assessment of environmental, economic and social benefits.
  7. Assessment of contribution to restoring carbon content in soil, increasing nutrients, revitalising marginal lands or ensuring food security (if applicable).

item.description:

item.title: ELEVATEX Open Call 2026
item.description:
This Call for Proposal is supported by the ELEVATEX Project Consortium and is co-funded by the European Union, under the Joint Cluster Initiative – Euroclusters – call, managed by the European Innovation Council and SMEs Executive Agency (EISMEA).

The 1st ELEVATEX Open Call aims to support individual textile SMEs developing innovative products, processes or services with clear market potential.

Projects should contribute to any of the following items:

  1. Product or service development
  2. New or improved processes for the green and/or digital transition
  3. Market launch preparation
  4. Strengthening SME competitiveness

Projects may focus on:

  1. Development of new or significantly improved products
  2. Development of innovative services
  3. Preparation for market entry

Projects must demonstrate:

  1. Impact and ambition: added value of the proposal, market potential, degree of novelty
  2. Excellence /expertise: Technical expertise and experience, availability of resources, risk management
  3. Implementation: project design, workplan coherence, feasibility of the project
  4. Coherence of the private contribution of the applicant

Master School and Fellowship Open Call 2027
item.description:
EIT Urban Mobility invites universities, academic consortia, and education-focused innovators to submit proposals to its Master School and Fellowship Open Call 2027, designed to accelerate the development and delivery of high-quality learning experiences that build the skills and capabilities needed for sustainable urban mobility. 

This Call focuses on a series of specific activity areas aimed at meeting the objectives of the EIT Urban Mobility Master School and the EIT Urban Mobility Innovation & Entrepreneurship (I&E) Fellowship. The Call supports proposals that contribute to closing the knowledge gap within urban mobility by offering high-quality, challenge-based, intersectoral, and international education. 

We are seeking proposals that align with the key objectives of the Master School and I&E Fellowship, including fostering innovation and entrepreneurship skills, increasing student and graduate volumes, strengthening links to industry and cities, and supporting startup creation through EIT-labelled programmes. 

Proposals for the Master School and Fellowship Open Call 2027 may be submitted by individual applicants or consortia, depending on the requirements for each activity type, as detailed in the Call Manual. 

The following five types of activities are eligible under this Call: 

MSA1: Summer schools for Master students  

MSA2: New EIT Urban Mobility Master programmes  

MSA3: New universities joining the existing EIT Urban Mobility Master School programmes  

MSA4: I&E Master programmes that join the EIT Urban Mobility I&E Fellowship  

MSA5: Other educational activities contributing to KPI EITHE05.1 (startups created by students and graduates from EIT Labelled programmes) 

This Call supports activities that demonstrate clear alignment with the strategic objectives of EIT Urban Mobility’s education and training portfolio, ensure compliance with the EIT Label requirements and show strong potential for long-term impact, scalability, and financial sustainability.

COSMIC 2nd Open Call: AI & Data Solutions to Boost the Green Transition
item.description:
The COSMIC project has received funding from the European Union’s Horizon Europe research and innovation programme under Grant Agreement No. 101189676.

COSMIC is supporting the Green Deal objectives by harnessing artificial intelligence (AI) and big data for large-scale energy resource optimisation. The project brings together a diverse consortium of large industries, research institutions, public authorities, NGOs, and SMEs to co-develop impactful, scalable digital solutions that address key sustainability challenges.

Through two Open Calls, COSMIC is selecting a maximum of 27 SMEs to co-develop AI-powered services and technologies aimed at enhancing energy efficiency and resource optimisation.

The 2nd Open Call will support up to 13 SMEs, including start-ups, working on AI- and data-driven innovations aligned with COSMIC’s mission. Selected participants will join a 10-month support programme offering mentoring, solution development, pilot site integration, and business scaling support.

Each selected SME may receive up to €150,000 funding as a fixed lump sum. Funding conditions include:

  1. SMEs and start-ups can receive reimbursement of up to 70% of their eligible costs.
  2. Non-profit legal entities may be funded up to 100% of their eligible costs.

Beneficiaries selected under this Open Call will have the opportunity to collaborate with COSMIC’s Core Technical Facilitators (CTFs) and Core Social Facilitator (CFS), receiving technological support to integrate their AI- and data-driven solutions. These solutions may be validated within COSMIC’s large-scale pilots across six European countries: Spain, Belgium, Portugal, France, Ireland, and Finland. Selected proposals will be supported for real-world testing in industrial or urban environments.

Each selected entity will develop a solution addressing one of the specific challenges outlined in the COSMIC Technical Guidelines. These include but are not limited to: carbon footprint integration, community engagement, waste classification, and smart energy management.

Participation is open to individual SMEs (including startups) from EU Member States and Horizon Europe Associated Countries.

Proposals must be AI- and data-centric, with clear potential for scalability, integration into COSMIC’s infrastructure, and measurable environmental and social impact.

item.title: Promoting an enabling civic space
Raising awareness of and building capacity for the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights
Support to awareness raising about the Arctic communities, including Indigenous People
FS4Africa Open Call 2 (OC2) addressed to innovation hubs
item.description:
The implementation of the FS4Africa sub-projects will be divided in 3 phases: Design (M1), Development (M2-M6), and Validation (M7-M8 months). Each of the 3 phases will be completed with submission of a deliverable, which summarises the work done during that phase. The review process will follow each phase of the implementation. Once the deliverable is received, it will be assessed by the FS4Africa Open Call Advisory Board members. A request for the deliverable's update may occur with specific requirements for what should be modified. Based on the results of the review and acceptance of respected deliverables, payment will be made towards the subgrantees in the following modalities:

  1. Phase 1 - Design (1 month / February 2027: Payment of 20% of the total grant [Deliverable: Training Activity plan];
  2. Phase 2 - Development (5 months / March 2027 – July 2027): Payment of 50% of the total grant [Deliverable: Training Delivery Report];
  3. Phase 3 - Validation (2 months / August 2027 – September 2027): Payment of the final 30% of the total grant [Deliverable: Final Report of the training activities].

Each Open Call 2 proposal must propose and implement a structured programme consisting of at least six (6) training–mentoring activities during the implementation period.

The required activities must include:

A. Support to Open Call 1 (OC1) Beneficiaries

Applicants must deliver at least two (2) training activities to support at least two (2) distinct Open Call 1 beneficiaries i.e. sub-projects (minimum two trainings in total, joint or dedicated sessions permitted). The two activities must involve at least two different OC1 sub-projects (i.e. not both activities mapped to the same OC1 beneficiary). The content should be tailored to the needs of the supported OC1 sub-projects and aligned with the relevant food safety challenges.

B. Support to FS4Africa Use Cases

Applicants must deliver at least four (4) training–mentoring activities targeting actors involved in one or more FS4Africa Use Cases in collaboration with other OC2 projects. Following selection, Open Call 2 sub-projects will coordinate among themselves, with support from the FS4Africa Executive Board (WP Leaders) and the Open Call team, through structured information exchange and alignment meetings to ensure balanced thematic coverage across all four FS4Africa Use Cases. Collaboration among OC2 sub-projects implies structured coordination of thematic focus, training design, delivery schedules, or mentoring approaches to ensure complementarity, avoid duplication, and maximise collective coverage of FS4Africa Use Cases.

Training–mentoring activities may take the form of online courses, webinars, interactive workshops, mentoring clinics, or other structured capacity-building formats. Joint or co-organised activities between OC2 sub-projects are optional but encouraged. Where such joint activities are implemented, they may count towards the minimum number (at least four) of required training–mentoring activities for each participating sub-project, provided that each sub-project’s role and contribution are clearly defined.

item.title: Rule of Law (ROLAW III)
RESIST II PROCESS INNOVATION OPEN CALL
item.description:
Project cost categories may include:

AI4Gov-X Cascade Funding Call - first edition [AI4Gov-X-FSTP-CALL-01-2026]
item.description:
The procedure follows the principles of:

  1. transparency,
  2. equal treatment,
  3. confidentiality,
  4. absence of conflict of interest.

Admissibility and eligibility checks precede scoring; only eligible applications will be scored.

Eligible proposals are ranked by total score within the available budget.

The final award decision is endorsed by POLI.design. All applicants will receive a written justification for the decision, with terms to appeal.

Selected beneficiaries will be requested to sign a Financial Support Agreement specifying the supported activity, eligible costs, maximum amount, payment schedule and reporting obligations.

Failure to sign within the indicated timeframe may lead to loss of the grant by the autonomous decision of POLI.design.

Payments will be made by POLI.design, normally after participation in the activity and approval of the beneficiary report and cost statement. Details are provided in the individual Financial Support Agreement (including e.g. the possibility of small prefinancing in justified cases).

Open Call for innovative projects enhancing the valorisation of agri-food biomass
item.description:
This call targets innovative SMEs developing solutions that increase the valorisation of biomass, contributing to more sustainable and resilient agri-food ecosystems through the development of new processes, services and products.

This Innovation Call includes financial support for projects especially focusing on the development, adoption, and commercialisation of innovative upcycled ingredients for food and non-food applications, products, business processes, and technologies (including digital) that maximise the valorisation of agri-food biomass and side-streams. These projects should have a minimum Technology Readiness Level (TRL) of 6.

1.The Innovation Open Call targets:

  1. SMEs in the agri-food production and/or processing sector;
  2. SMEs in other sectors (ex. cosmetics, nutraceuticals, etc) that valorise biomass/by-products/side-streams from the agri-food sector;
  3. SMEs in other sectors (ex. cosmetics, nutraceuticals, etc) that valorise their own biomass/by-products/side-streams for the agri-food sector;
  4. SMEs offering solutions and/or technology for the valorisation of biomass/by-products/side-streams from the agri-food sector (development and application of new technologies or processes).

2.The applicant’s project must lead to the implementation of either a new process, a new product, or a new service or a combination of them.

3.Proposals can be submitted either by a single SME or by an SMEs consortium with up to three (3) SMEs.

4.Applicants can be SMEs registered at EU Member States and countries associated to the Single Market Programme.

5.Projects must have a minimum Technology Readiness Level (TRL) of at least 6.

6.For Single SME projects, the maximum lump-sum grant per project (1 SME) is €60.000.

7.For collaborative projects, the maximum lump-sum grant per project (2-3 SMEs) is €90.000.

8.Innovation grants must be matched by at least 15% private financial contribution (debt or own financial resources), with proof required.

item.title: Enhancing the Cybersecurity Capabilities of the Domestic SME Sector (SSNS-NCC-HU-FSTP-2026)
item.description:
WHO CAN SUBMIT A GRANT APPLICATION?

Micro, small and medium-sized enterprises may submit grant applications for the call if they:

  1. have at least three closed financial years (each supported by financial statements and covering full 365-day period of operation as a pre-company is not included);
  2. operate in the sectors listed in Annexes 2 or 3 of the Cybersecurity Act

WHAT ACTIVITIES ARE ELIGIBLE FOR SUPPORT?

  1. Acquisition of new equipment and intangible assets resulting in the enhancement of cyber resilience
  2. Centralised endpoint protection solutions (traditional antivirus);
  3. Advanced endpoint protection and incident detection solutions (EDR/XDR);
  4. Perimeter protection solutions (firewall, Web Application Firewall, proxy, VPN gateway);
  5. Network traffic monitoring and intrusion detection solutions (WAF, IDS/IPS, NDR);
  6. Solutions against denial-of-service attacks (Anti-DDoS);
  7. Data backup and recovery solutions (backup/restore);
  8. Centralised log collection solutions (log management);
  9. Advanced log analysis solutions;
  10. Security information and event management solutions (SIEM);
  11. Mobile device management solutions (MDM);
  12. Multi-factor authentication solutions (MFA);
  13. Identity and access a management solutions (IAM);
  14. Data loss prevention solutions (DLP);
  15. Anti-spam and e-mail security solutions;
  16. Governance, risk and compliance management solutions (GRC);
  17. Encryption and key management solutions;
  18. Replacement of hardware or software components that are no longer supported by security update (obsolete hardware/software).
  19. Procurement of Services related to the enhancement of cyber resilience:
  20. Cybersecurity testing and assessment services (vulnerability testing);
  21. Cybersecurity Operations Centre (SOC) services;
  22. Cybersecurity-related training activities;
  23. Subscriptions to cybersecurity training platforms;
  24. Integration of hardware and software tools (system design, implementation, configuration, testing and documentation);
  25. Cybersecurity-related consulting and professional advisory services.

Details are included in the call for tenders!

FuseNet Call for the Organisation of Mini-Workshops in Fusion
item.description: As part of its activities, FuseNet supports the FuseNet member institutions with the organisation of mini-workshops. These are small-scale workshops that aim to connect young talents with experienced researchers. They have the set-up of masterclasses on a dedicated topic, include interaction between experts and students and are organised for either MSc or PhD students. Such events typically last 2–5 days. This call is for mini-workshops that are to be organised before the end of this year.

item.title: FuseNet Support of Master Students going on an internship abroad
item.description:
This support package is meant to facilitate MSc students in fusion who want to go abroad for an internship, thereby stimulating quality of education through exchanges. The internship can take place at a fusion research centre or university involved in fusion research. This place is not limited to Europe. Students registered at universities that are a FuseNet member and/or are doing their internship at hosts that are a FuseNet member, and their home university is located in one of the EUROfusion member states can apply for financial support for their internship here.

The financial support consists of a fixed amount of travel support (depending on the travel distance) and accommodation/living support (depending on the internship duration). The application has to be submitted at least 2 weeks before and within 6 months of the start of the internship. Other sources of funding for the internship (scholarships or grants, compensation or salary from the host institute, funds from other EU support schemes, etc.) need to be specified. Funding can also be restricted in cases where funding has been received from FuseNet in the past (see the terms and conditions on the FuseNet webpage).

item.title: FuseNet Support of Master Students in attending Educational Events
item.description:
This support scheme is launched to support and facilitate Master students to take part in educational activities external to their own organization. It allows Master students to participate in fusion Master classes, summer schools, and other short-term events with a dominant educational character. All conferences are excluded from support except when the Master student has his/her own contribution at the event (see terms and conditions for details).

Students registered at universities that are a FuseNet member and/or are seeking to attend educational activities at hosts that are a FuseNet member, and both the home university and the educational event are located in one of the countries that are part of the EUROfusion member states, can apply here for financial support to take part in a fusion-related educational training activity.

The financial support consists of a fixed amount of travel support (depending on the travel distance) and subsistence support (depending on the duration). The (training) activity has to take place at a university group or research institute in one of the EUROfusion members states or at a FuseNet member institute. The application has to be submitted at least 2 weeks before the actual start of the educational event. The educational event has to start no later than 6 months after application.

item.title: Call for CSOs initiatives “Code Success” Fund
item.description:
The Code Success Fund will support initiatives that raise awareness of EU rights and values, promote democracy and active civic participation, protect media freedom and counter disinformation, and work for equality, inclusion, and non-discrimination.

The supported initiatives may include:

-Awareness raising, including communication and information campaigns,

-Activities aimed at countering disinformation regarding EU values and policies,

-Various activities fostering civic dialogues at local level on topics related to EU rights and values,

-Activities in the local community to create coalitions and partnerships between civil society organizations,

-Civic participation/advocacy with the aim of contributing to decision-making processes (at regional or local level),

-Training, exchange of experience and good practices, mentoring, knowledge transfer, and other activities aimed at increasing the capacity of civil society organizations and other stakeholders on topics related to EU values,

-Activities that promote interethnic and intercultural dialogue in the community

-Providing free advice and monitoring implementation of EU policies, rights, and values at the local level in accordance with the EU Treaties and the Charter of Fundamental Rights.

Phortify Internship (Traineeship) Grants - Pilot Call
item.description:
About Phortify

Phortify - Photonics Education Network for Next-Gen Innovation and Digital Skills Excellence for Industry and Society - is dedicated to the Enhancement and Harmonisation of Photonics education and training across Europe. Phortify offers a two-year European Master’s in Photonics Engineering, combining advanced coursework, hands-on laboratory training, and cutting-edge digital skills development, structured mobility windows and industry placements. Students who complete the Phortify track receive an official Phortify Certificate in addition to their Master’s degree. Students can enrol in a Phortify-related master programme at any of the 7 participating universities. In addition, Phortify provides stand-alone modules with harmonised standards for quality assurance and certification, offering high flexibility and mobility for both students and industry professionals. Finally, Phortify organises mentoring programmes for women postdocs and early PIs as well as one-day immersive experience events for prospective students, providing a unique opportunity to explore photonics and its applications.

About the Phortify Funding Framework

Phortify’s Funding Framework aims to widen participation in photonics by supporting students and staff who are traditionally underrepresented in STEM. This includes women and gender minorities, students from low-income or socio-economically disadvantaged backgrounds, first-generation university students, and learners with disabilities or specific support needs. The programme also encourages participation from regions with limited access to advanced higher education, as well as those belonging to cultural or minority groups that may be underrepresented in higher education. By addressing these dimensions of diversity, Phortify helps ensure a more inclusive and equitable community of future photonics professionals. Participants selected for Phortify funding receive financial support for studies and mobility pathways within the participating master programmes, or for mentoring programmes and STEM experience workshops organised by one of the Phortify partners.

PowerBoost – unlock energy related challenges
item.description:
Under the PowerBoost stream, the only eligible activity is the implementation of an innovation audit, that will be reimbursed with a total of EUR 2000 once the audit is approved, aimed at identifying challenges and opportunities for the Applicant organisation to contribute to grid flexibility, sustainability, and digitalisation in the energy sector.

The innovation audit should help the SME understand its current processes, technologies, and operational gaps, and define areas where innovation or new technology adoption could improve efficiency, flexibility, or integration with renewable energy systems.

The innovation audit must result in a Challenge Description Report, prepared using the official template provided by GreenGrid.

Accelerating Food Sustainability - through Household Dietary Shifts, Trust and Transparency, and Innovations in Circular Food Processing Systems
item.description:
Central to the FutureFoodS Partnership is the ambition that tackling the complex challenges making current European food systems unsustainable requires mobilizing knowledge, actors and resources through a food systems approach. An approach of this kind is important as it propounds a holistic view when addressing wicked problems, acknowledging enduring dependencies between food system actors, as well as identifying drivers and leverage points within systems. The proposals submitted to this call should clearly delineate and characterise the particular food system under inquiry. What are its key actors? What are the main dependencies within the system? What levers may induce enhanced sustainability of the system? How does the chosen one connect to other food systems?

Applicants shall demonstrate the originality of their approach and build on the results of existing EU level initiatives. Funded projects shall foster synergies with other initiatives and shall have the capacities to take a changing policy environment into consideration - also during the life-time of the project.

Each application submitted under this call should focus on one of the three call topics listed below. The topic chosen should be clearly indicated in the proposal:

1 Domestic food practices for enhancing sustainable and healthy diets

2 Towards diverse, sustainable and circular food processing systems

3 Importance of trust and transparency

In addition, the type of projects, Exploratory Research Project or Accelerating Innovation Project, chosen should be clearly indicated in the proposal.


Please note that primary production (on land and sea/water), such as growing food, agricultural production and other specific aspects related to it, as well as diseases related to nutrition and lifestyle are not in focus in this partnership. These aspects are covered in other Horizon Europe partnerships. However, dependencies and impacts related to these aspects may need to be reflected upon in a project to fully embrace a food systems approach.


The Call Announcement highlights the aim, expected approach and topics of the call. It also details the countries/regions participating in funding the call, the eligibility criteria, submission, evaluation and selection procedures, and modalities for projects funding and reporting.

Specific funding regulations apply depending on the funding organisations: each partner applying to the call must ensure that its contribution to the overall project conforms to these regulations, as reported in the document “Annex I - National/regional regulations” available for download on the submission platform.

item.title: DIGITWIN4CIUE Reduction-Fees Scholarships
DIGITWIN4CIUE project-Scholarships for the Executive Master in Digital Twins on Infrastructures and Cities
AMU Scholarships for the internship of the Master 2 « Biology & Health », study-track « AI and biomarkers » within the European Project « Advanced Digital Skills AI and Health ».
item.description:
The internship period must be in line with the objective of the Master :

- a complete understanding on how AI is used in biomedical and healthcare research,

- a better identification, a controlled development and validation of biomarkers used for prevention, diagnosis or monitoring diseases,

- a more appropriate use of Information Technology tools and methods to analyse the data to exploit the full potential of large datasets from medical and biological research,

- the latest AI operating techniques, such as machine learning and deep learning, to propose innovative solution in personalised medicine,

- designing, planing and conducting high-impact research,

- the participation in interdisciplinary research project involving skills in biology, AI and bio-informatics, by identifying specificlly required input from each main scientific area to come up with innovative solutions in the health field.
